corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: The model is fully probabilistic and autoregressive, with the predictive distribution for each audio sample conditioned on all previous ones; nonetheless we show that it can be efficiently trained on data with tens of thousands of samples per second of audio.
Modeling joint probabilities over pixels or words using neural architectures as products of conditional distributions yields state-of-the-art generation.

The question this paper addresses is whether similar approaches can succeed in generating wideband raw audio waveforms, which are signals with very high temporal resolution, at least 16,000 samples per second (see Fig. ). • We show that WaveNets can generate raw speech signals with subjective naturalness never before reported in the field of text-to-speech (TTS), as assessed by human raters. • In order to deal with long-range temporal dependencies needed for raw audio generation, we develop new architectures based on dilated causal convolutions, which exhibit very large receptive fields. • We show that when conditioned on a speaker identity, a single model can be used to generate different voices. • The same architecture shows strong results when tested on a small speech recognition dataset, and is promising when used to generate other audio modalities such as music.

The question this paper addresses is whether similar approaches can succeed in generating wideband raw audio waveforms, which are signals with very high temporal resolution, at least 16,000 samples per second (see Fig. ). • We show that WaveNets can generate raw speech signals with subjective naturalness never before reported in the field of text-to-speech (TTS), as assessed by human raters. • In order to deal with long-range temporal dependencies needed for raw audio generation, we develop new architectures based on dilated causal convolutions, which exhibit very large receptive fields. • We show that when conditioned on a speaker identity, a single model can be used to generate different voices. • The same architecture shows strong results when tested on a small speech recognition dataset, and is promising when used to generate other audio modalities such as music.

The question this paper addresses is whether similar approaches can succeed in generating wideband raw audio waveforms, which are signals with very high temporal resolution, at least 16,000 samples per second (see Fig. ). • We show that WaveNets can generate raw speech signals with subjective naturalness never before reported in the field of text-to-speech (TTS), as assessed by human raters. • In order to deal with long-range temporal dependencies needed for raw audio generation, we develop new architectures based on dilated causal convolutions, which exhibit very large receptive fields. • We show that when conditioned on a speaker identity, a single model can be used to generate different voices. • The same architecture shows strong results when tested on a small speech recognition dataset, and is promising when used to generate other audio modalities such as music.
We believe that WaveNets provide a generic and flexible framework for tackling many applications that rely on audio generation (e.g.

However, its subjective naturalness is often significantly worse than that of the concatenative approach; synthesized speech often sounds muffled and has artifacts. reported three major factors that can degrade the subjective naturalness; quality of vocoders, accuracy of generative models, and effect of oversmoothing.

There have been a number of attempts to address these issues individually, such as developing high-quality vocoders , improving the accuracy of generative models , and compensating the oversmoothing effect . showed that state-of-the-art statistical parametric speech syntheziers matched state-of-the-art concatenative ones in some languages.

There have been a number of attempts to address these issues individually, such as developing high-quality vocoders , improving the accuracy of generative models , and compensating the oversmoothing effect . showed that state-of-the-art statistical parametric speech syntheziers matched state-of-the-art concatenative ones in some languages.
When applied to TTS, WaveNets produced samples that outperform the current best TTS systems in subjective naturalness.
